issue_title: issue on master with the announce/withdraw command
issue_body:
Reported by mail:

If I announce for example the following route :
`announce route 10.55.130/24 next-hop 100::1 community [ 15879:666  ]`

and do a 'show routes', I can see the prefix :
`neighbor 213.133.37.226 10.55.130.0/24 next-hop 100::1`
`neighbor 213.133.37.227 10.55.130.0/24 next-hop 100::1`

However, I'm unable to withdraw it....

I tried for example :

`withdraw 10.55.130.0/24`
`withdraw 10.55.130.0/24 next-hop 100::1`

But the route simply stays....  I can only restart ExaBGP to clear it.

Here some output from the log :
```
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:06 3473   processes     Command from process jafar-exabgp-tcpserver : withdraw route 10.55.130.0/24
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:06 3473   reactor       callback | handling 'withdraw route 10.55.130.0/24' with withdraw_route
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:06 3473   reactor       callback | installing withdraw_route
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:06 3473   reactor       callback | running
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:06 3473   configuration . route            | '10.55.130.0/24'
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:06 3473   reactor       route removed from neighbor 213.133.37.226 local-ip 213.133.37.229 local-as 65099 peer-as 15879 router-id 213.133.37.229 family-allowed in-open, neighbor 213.133.37.227 local-ip 213.133.37.229 local-as 65099 peer-as 15879 router-id 213.133.37.229 family-allowed in-open : 10.55.130.0/24
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:06 3473   reactor       responding to jafar-exabgp-tcpserver : done
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:06 3473   reactor       callback | removing
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:07 3473   reactor       performing dynamic route update
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:07 3473   reactor       updated peers dynamic routes successfully
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   processes     Command from process jafar-exabgp-tcpserver : show routes
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       callback | handling 'show routes' with show_routes
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       callback | installing show_routes
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       callback | running
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       responding to jafar-exabgp-tcpserver : neighbor 213.133.37.226 10.55.131.0/24 next-hop 213.133.37.229
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       responding to jafar-exabgp-tcpserver : neighbor 213.133.37.226 10.55.130.0/24 next-hop 100::1
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       callback | running
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       responding to jafar-exabgp-tcpserver : neighbor 213.133.37.227 10.55.131.0/24 next-hop 213.133.37.229
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       responding to jafar-exabgp-tcpserver : neighbor 213.133.37.227 10.55.130.0/24 next-hop 100::1
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       callback | running
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       responding to jafar-exabgp-tcpserver : done
Thu, 30 Jun 2016 17:23:16 3473   reactor       callback | removing
```

The next-hop specified is not valid in this case obviously, because it's an IPv6 next-hop.
So I think either 'announce' should deny it, or withdraw should function.
(Not 100% sure if the IPv6 next-hop can have a valid use case for an IPv4 prefix)

Also, the reason I came across this issue is because I did some testing with advertising IPv6 prefixes to IPv4 neighbors (family inet + family inet6),
and was struggling with the next-hop to specify in the announcement in this case...

If I specify the IPv4-address or self, the session with the neighbor (JunOS) seems to break.

Any suggestions on how to accomplish this ? (it's an eBGP session in this case for that matter)

I also still haven't found a solution that if a child process is terminated/killed,
that ExaBGP 4.x does not restart it. In 3.x, it simply did that.
I fiddled with 'respawn = true' for exabgp.api, but it just does not restart.

Same thing is that if I kill my own 'tcpserver' process (written in Perl),
that process becomes defunct.

issue_title: Connection still up after 'state -> down' notification
issue_body:
Hi Thomas,

We found a peer which triggered the following log message:
```
Oct  3 08:57:51 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   reactor       New peer setup: neighbor 196.46.25.216 local-ip 196.46.25.86 local-as 12654 peer-as 2018 router-id 196.46.25.86 family-allowed in-open
Oct  3 08:57:51 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   reactor       New peer setup: neighbor 2001:43f8:6d0::216 local-ip 2001:43f8:6d0::86 local-as 12654 peer-as 2018 router-id 196.46.25.86 family-allowed in-open
Oct  3 08:57:54 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   network       Connected to peer neighbor 196.46.25.216 local-ip 196.46.25.86 local-as 12654 peer-as 2018 router-id 196.46.25.86 family-allowed in-open (out)
Oct  3 08:57:54 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   network       Connected to peer neighbor 2001:43f8:6d0::216 local-ip 2001:43f8:6d0::86 local-as 12654 peer-as 2018 router-id 196.46.25.86 family-allowed in-open (out)
Oct  4 00:48:47 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   network       Peer   196.46.25.216 ASN 2018    automatic protocol cleanup
Oct  4 00:48:47 rrc19 exabgp: 4673   network       Peer 2001:43f8:6d0::216 ASN 2018    automatic protocol cleanup
```
whatever this event at Oct  4 00:48:47 was, it sent the following to client process:
```
{
  "neighbor": {
    "reason": "automatic protocol cleanup",
    "state": "down",
    "asn": {
      "peer": "2018",
      "local": "12654"
    },
    "address": {
      "peer": "196.46.25.216",
      "local": "196.46.25.86"
    },
    "ip": "196.46.25.216"
  },
  "type": "state",
  "counter": 3837054,
  "ppid": "1",
  "pid": "4673",
  "host": "rrc19.ripe.net",
  "time": 1475542127.33,
  "exabgp": "3.4.8"
}

{
  "neighbor": {
    "reason": "automatic protocol cleanup",
    "state": "down",
    "asn": {
      "peer": "2018",
      "local": "12654"
    },
    "address": {
      "peer": "2001:43f8:6d0::216",
      "local": "2001:43f8:6d0::86"
    },
    "ip": "2001:43f8:6d0::216"
  },
  "type": "state",
  "counter": 8379281,
  "ppid": "1",
  "pid": "4673",
  "host": "rrc19.ripe.net",
  "time": 1475542127.33,
  "exabgp": "3.4.8"
}
```
But the connection stayed up, and we continue to receive update messages from the peer, these are still being passed onto the client process.

This confused our RIB state processing engine as the last 'state' change for the peer was 'down'.

Do you have any idea how this might have happened? It looks like this originates from Protocol.close, but that looks like it should kill the connection, so we don't understand how the connection is still up :)
(v.3.4.15)

Thanks!
Colin

issue_title: AS-path / Extended length handling problem
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm encountering a problem with as-path handling:

```
...| message       | Peer       192.0.2.6 ASN 65539   << UPDATE
...| parser        | parsing UPDATE (  49) 0000 0028 4001 0100 5002 000A 0202 0001 0003 0001 0002 4003 04C0 0002 06C0 200C 0001 0002 0000 0001 0000 0001 20CB 0071 1F
...| parser        | withdrawn NLRI none
...| parser        | attribute origin             flag 0x40 type 0x01 len 0x01 payload 00
...| message       | Peer       192.0.2.6 ASN 65539   Could not decode message "2"
...| message       | Peer       192.0.2.6 ASN 65539   unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'int' and 'str'
...| message       | Traceback (most recent call last):
...| message       |   File "/root/exabgp-master/lib/exabgp/reactor/protocol.py", line 208, in read_message
...| message       |     message = Message.unpack(msg_id,body,self.negotiated)
...| message       |   File "/root/exabgp-master/lib/exabgp/bgp/message/message.py", line 181, in unpack
...| message       |     return cls.klass(message).unpack_message(data,negotiated)
...| message       |   File "/root/exabgp-master/lib/exabgp/bgp/message/update/__init__.py", line 269, in unpack_message
...| message       |     attributes = Attributes.unpack(_attributes,negotiated)
...| message       |   File "/root/exabgp-master/lib/exabgp/bgp/message/update/attribute/attributes.py", line 274, in unpack
...| message       |     attributes = cls().parse(data,negotiated)
...| message       |   File "/root/exabgp-master/lib/exabgp/bgp/message/update/attribute/attributes.py", line 366, in parse
...| message       |     return self.parse(left,negotiated)
...| message       |   File "/root/exabgp-master/lib/exabgp/bgp/message/update/attribute/attributes.py", line 321, in parse
...| message       |     length = (length << 8) + data[3]
...| message       | TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'int' and 'str'
...| message       |
...| network       | Peer       192.0.2.3 ASN 65537   connection to 192.0.2.3:179 failed
...| message       | Peer       192.0.2.6 ASN 65539   >> NOTIFICATION (1,0,"can not decode update message of type "2"")
...| network       | Peer       192.0.2.6 ASN 65539   out loop, peer reset, message [notification sent (1,0)] error[Message header error / Unspecific / can not decode update message of type "2"]
```

It seems that `data[3]` is a string while it should be an int: I "fixed" it by doing this (just to have a working version of the `.parse` for this scenario):

```
length = (length << 8) + ord(data[3])
```

(but please don't try it at home!)

```
...| routes        | peer 192.0.2.6 ASN 65539   << UPDATE (4) nlri  (   5) 203.0.113.35/32 next-hop 192.0.2.6
...| message       | Peer       192.0.2.6 ASN 65539   << UPDATE

...| parser        | withdrawn NLRI none
...| parser        | attribute origin             flag 0x40 type 0x01 len 0x01 payload 00
...| parser        | attribute as-path            flag 0x50 type 0x02 len 0x0a payload 0202 0001 0003 0001 0002
```

docker-py is encoding `X-Registry-Auth` with regular base64 and not the url safe version of base64 that jwt tokens use.

issue_title: Unable to allow "file://" as allowed_origin for WebSocket
Unicode chars break the library when WAMP Debug is used with JSON Serialization
issue_body:
So I found this kindof subtle issue.  Specifically, when `self.logFrames = True`, the `logTxFrames` and `logRxFrames` methods in `autobahn/websocket/protocol.py` call `binascii.b2a_hex` on the frame payload.  This is fine when the payload is msgpack binary since that has been encoded into a byte string already.  However, when the payload is JSON it is a unicode payload which gets implicitly converted to a byte string using the ascii codec.  As a result, if there is a unicode character in there (for example `u"\u2639"`) an exception is thrown from the logger that looks like this:

```Shell
2016-02-09T12:29:21-0600 Unhandled error in Deferred:
2016-02-09T12:29:21-0600 Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/prak/anaconda/envs/bonetest/lib/python2.7/site-packages/autobahn/wamp/websocket.py", line 95, in onMessage
    self._session.onMessage(msg)
  File "/Users/prak/anaconda/envs/bonetest/lib/python2.7/site-packages/autobahn/wamp/protocol.py", line 639, in onMessage
    txaio.add_callbacks(on_reply, success, error)
  File "/Users/prak/anaconda/envs/bonetest/lib/python2.7/site-packages/txaio/tx.py", line 392, in add_callbacks
    future.addCallbacks(callback, errback)
  File "/Users/prak/anaconda/envs/bonetest/lib/python2.7/site-packages/twisted/internet/defer.py", line 306, in addCallbacks
    self._runCallbacks()
--- <exception caught here> ---
  File "/Users/prak/anaconda/envs/bonetest/lib/python2.7/site-packages/twisted/internet/defer.py", line 588, in _runCallbacks
    current.result = callback(current.result, *args, **kw)
  File "/Users/prak/anaconda/envs/bonetest/lib/python2.7/site-packages/autobahn/wamp/protocol.py", line 601, in success
    self._transport.send(reply)
  File "/Users/prak/anaconda/envs/bonetest/lib/python2.7/site-packages/autobahn/wamp/websocket.py", line 123, in send
    self.sendMessage(payload, isBinary)
  File "/Users/prak/anaconda/envs/bonetest/lib/python2.7/site-packages/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py", line 2211, in sendMessage
    self.sendFrame(opcode=opcode, payload=payload, sync=sync, rsv=4 if sendCompressed else 0)
  File "/Users/prak/anaconda/envs/bonetest/lib/python2.7/site-packages/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py", line 1836, in sendFrame
    self.logTxFrame(frameHeader, payload, payload_len, chopsize, sync)
  File "/Users/prak/anaconda/envs/bonetest/lib/python2.7/site-packages/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py", line 1164, in logTxFrame
    self.log.debug("TX Frame to %s : fin = %s, rsv = %s, opcode = %s, mask = %s, length = %s, repeat_length = %s, chopsize = %s, sync = %s, payload = %s" % info)
exceptions.UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe2 in position 11: ordinal not in range(128)
```

I replicated this in an isolated case by modifying the `rpc/arguments` example, enabling debugging and adding in that unicode character to the payload.  I used the bonefish router to test because it was easy to disable msgpack on the command line (and I had a build handy) but this exception is post-invocation and pre-response so it would happen in any router.  Disabling debug mode makes things work again.  This is tested on autobahn v0.12.1 (the latest on pypi), Python 2.7, using Twisted, though likely applies to other cases.

Any thoughts on how to best address it?  Thanks.

issue_title: (Custom) domain with no certificate still serves https
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:
- Install deis v 1.13
- Create 2 applications (a and b)
- Add a custom domain to both applications (a.example.org and b.example.org)
- Add a https certificate to one of the domains (b.example.org)
- Access https://a.example.org

See that the connection is accepted but served with the certificate for b.example.org. After accepting the exception of the invalid certificate in your browser traffic will be served by application B instead of application A.

An example can be seen in https://fiesta-mundial.beeple.eu/ vs http://fiesta-mundial.beeple.eu/ 

I am not sure what an acceptable solution would be, but I would suggest either a "connection not accepted / reset" or if this is not possible in nginx router configuration a 404 when accessing the domain over HTTPs if no certificate is set.

I have not tested this but either this also will occur in deis/router (deis V2); or an updated nginx configuration should be relatively easily translatable to Deis V1 as the only significant change in router component (AFAIK) is how the config is generated and not the possibilities of the configuration (at least as far as HTTPs is concerned)

issue_title: Store and use access tokens in the configuration file
issue_body:
Currently every command invocation fetches a new access token from the stored refresh token. I think this is going to quickly get out of hand if and when people start making a lot of CLI calls.

Storing access tokens (and their expiration time) in the configuration file will allow us to make calls directly instead of needing to always go and get a token. Doing this should have the added benefit of making each CLI command more responsive.

I'm thinking we add the following new keys:

`<service>_access_token`
`<service>_access_token_expires_at`

issue_title: Add go-test to the travis-CI
Ability to incrementally build a block
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

This PR added optimizations to the process of importing blocks https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/565

The underlying changes to `apply_transaction` are causing problems in eth-tester.  Now, there is no way to leave the chain in a *half* state with some transactions applied to the HEAD block.  This removes the ability to incrementally apply transactions to a block without digging into internals.

### How can it be fixed

Not sure but:

- Need a way for `import_block` to delay building the transaction trie until the end of the block.
- Need a way to do what the previous `apply_transaction` functionality did, executing the transaction and fully updating the pending block with the latest *state*.

issue_title: Trinity Alpha Release Checklist
issue_body:
### Definition of what's needed for alpha release

All of the following need to work for our alpha release.

- full sync
  - [ ] `$ trinity`
    - initializes databases and runs full sync on main chain
    - starts with fast sync, switches to full sync when done
  - `$ trinity --ropsten`
    - same as previous but on the ropsten network.
- light sync
  - `$ trinity --light`
    - initializes separate light database, syncs header chain
- rpc
    - JSON-RPC server over IPC socket should be run in both full and light modes.  
    - All standard `eth_` endpoints should work or explicitely be stubbed out returning an error response indicating that it hasn't yet been implemented.
- console
  - `$ trinity attach`
    - Attaches to a running trinity node, exposes a python REPL with a Web3 instance available in the context.

issue_title: web3.eth.account signature hypothesis tests
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

Want to catch/prevent more issues like #466 earlier.

### How can it be fixed?

More hypothesis tests. Piper's suggestions (on #477):

* A signed transaction's signature recovers to the expected sender address
* The raw transaction can be rlp decoded into a transaction object
* An rlp decoded transaction object's v,r,s match the v,r,s from the signature.
* The signature bytes are 65 bytes in length

The downside is that they can be pretty time-intensive. Just a test for the length of signature bytes added 7 seconds to a 53-second test suite.

Example:
```
--- a/tests/core/eth-module/test_accounts.py
+++ b/tests/core/eth-module/test_accounts.py
@@ -2,10 +2,19 @@
 
 import pytest
 
+from eth_keys.constants import (
+    SECPK1_N,
+)
 from eth_utils import (
     is_checksum_address,
 )
 
+from hypothesis import (
+    example,
+    given,
+    strategies as st,
+)
+
 from web3 import (
     Account,
     Web3,
@@ -268,6 +277,16 @@ def test_eth_account_sign(acct, message, key, expected_bytes, expected_hash, v,
     assert account.sign(message_text=message) == signed
 
 
+@given(st.binary(), st.integers(
+    min_value=Web3.toInt(hexstr='0x1' + '00' * 31),
+    max_value=SECPK1_N - 1
+    )
+)
+def test_message_signature_length(acct, message, key):
+    signed = acct.sign(message, key)
+    assert len(signed.signature) == 65
+
+
 @pytest.mark.parametrize(
     'txn, private_key, expected_raw_tx, tx_hash, r, s, v',
     (
```

issue_body:
<!--
Hi!  Read this; it's important.

This is an issue tracker for conda -- the package manager.  File feature requests
for conda here, as well as bug reports about something conda has messed up.

If your issue is a bug report or feature request for:
  * a specific conda package from Anaconda ('defaults' channel):
      ==> file at https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues
  * a specific conda package from conda-forge:
      ==> file at the corresponding feedstock under https://github.com/conda-forge
  * repo.anaconda.com access and service:
      ==> file at https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues
  * anaconda.org access and service:
      ==> file at https://anaconda.org/contact/report
  * commands under 'conda build':
      ==> file at https://github.com/conda/conda-build
  * commands under 'conda env':
      ==> please file it here!
  * all other conda commands that start with 'conda':
      ==> please file it here!

If you continue on, and especially if you are submitting a bug report,

   **please include all requested information below.**

If a maintainer determines the information is required to understand 
your issue, and if it is not provided, your issue may be
closed automatically.

-->

**I'm submitting a...**
  - [ ] bug report
  - [X] feature request


## Current Behavior
<!-- What actually happens?
     If you want to include console output, please use "Steps to Reproduce" below. -->

According to the [docs](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/tasks/manage-pkgs.html#preventing-packages-from-updating-pinning) it is possible to pin a list of packages in a conda environment by adding a file named `pinned` to the `conda-meta` directory with a list of the packages that you do not want updated.

### Steps to Reproduce
<!-- If the current behavior is a bug, please provide specific, minimal steps to independently reproduce.
     Include the exact conda commands that reproduce the issue and their output between the ticks below. -->
```
N/A
```

## Expected Behavior
<!-- What do you think should happen? -->

Is it possible to pin all packages in a conda environment without explicitly mentioning all of them?  i.e. just use a keyword like `all` in the `pinned` file inside the `conda-meta` directory.

issue_title: "conda env remove" will delete a directory named like the environment
issue_body:
This is, I think, a very unfortunate bug of conda-env:
```sh
mkdir -p test/i-do-not-want-to-delete-this-dir
cd test
conda env remove -n i-do-not-want-to-delete-this-dir
```
conda goes wild and deletes the ```i-do-not-want-to-delete-this-dir``` directory.

I'm on these versions of conda/conda-env:
```
conda                     4.1.12                   py27_0    conda-forge
conda-env                 2.5.2                    py27_0    conda-forge
```

issue_title: channel UNC path encoding errors on windows
issue_body:
I have some channel specifications for UNC file paths on windows that stopped working in conda 4.2.11, but did work in 4.2.9 and earlier

The conda command with channel specification followed this style
conda install -c file://\\machine\shared_folder\path\conda  my_package

Channels specified in condarc were also affected and caused a bug when parsing the condarc file (via calling a conda command).

Is there another preferred style for UNC paths on windows?


I see some new channel decoding code in the channel model that is likely the culprit.  
if value.startswith('file:') and on_win:	
	value = value.replace('\\', '/')	
        return Channel.from_url(value)

issue_title: User stories for "features" in conda
issue_body:
### As a conda user:

- I want conda to find the optimal binary packages for my cpu and gpu. 
- I want the ability to choose between gpu and cpu versions of a package.
- I want to pick a specific package from a variety of cpu-optimized packages, so that I can have explicit specificity of my environment. 
- I want to create a debug environment. (mutex on Windows but not on others)
- I want to choose the python interpreter from a variety of alternatives (e.g. cpython, pypy).
- I want user-friendly expressiveness.

      conda install python=2.7 py-interpreter=cpython
      conda install python=2.7 py-interpreter=pypy
      conda install numpy blas=openblas gpu=cuda8

- I want tools to be able to expand information contained in the hash of a build string, for example to see what cuda versions are available for a particular package.

### As a package recipe author:

- I want a way to force exclusivity of packages other than package name, specifically for when I know there will be overlapping paths and functionality.
- I want to have a clear mental model of the difference between when my package needs to depend on another package and when my package needs to declare a "feature" requirement.

issue_title: noarch python entry points created by conda differ than those created by conda-build
issue_body:
The entry point scripts which are created by conda for `noarch: python` packages differ slightly from those created for standard packages by conda-build.  The templates for the scripts are:

[conda](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/gateways/disk/create.py#L37-L43):
``` python
python_entry_point_template = dals("""
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
if __name__ == '__main__':
    from sys import exit
    from %(module)s import %(func)s
    exit(%(func)s())
""")
```

[conda-build](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/master/conda_build/utils.py#L61-L67):
``` python
PY_TMPL = """\
if __name__ == '__main__':
    import sys
    import %(module)s
    sys.exit(%(module)s.%(func)s())
"""
```
I've found at least one example where the template used by conda produces a script which does not work but when used with the conda-build template the script run fine.

issue_title: conda should prefer the latest version from the request channel
issue_body:
Scenario:

hypothetical package call `pyconstants`.

`bcollins` channel has pyconstants 0.0.2 and 0.0.1
`dmertz` channel has pyconstants 0.0.1

`dmertz` installs `conda install -c bcollins pyconstants` and gets 0.0.2
`dmertz` decides he doesn't trust `bcollins` and runs `conda install -c dmertz pyconstants`

right now this says "pyconstants" already installed, instead of automatically using the latest version from `dmertz` which is `0.0.1`

issue_title: Blockiterating "Advanced" Dtypes --> Segfault

Creating a bcolz table with an "advanced" (mixed higher dimensions) dtype works.
Iterating it with the bcolz.iterblocks() function results in segmentation fault.
Depending on the exact composition of dtype, there is an python error before the segmentation fault (see after minimal example).
I am a new bcolz user and therefore not sure if my code should be working (and this is a bug) or if this kind of dtypes are not supported and these errors are just the consequence of wrong usage.

import bcolz
import numpy as np

dtype = np.dtype([

arr = np.empty(1000, dtype=dtype)

issue_title: Exception logged in Windows from select() due to Rejected event
issue_body:
The following trivial script results in a `log.exception()` message in Windows, which doesn't seem like the expected behavior.

```
import lomond

ws = lomond.WebSocket('ws://www.google.com')
events = list(ws.connect())
print(events)
```

Output:
```
[Connecting(url='ws://www.google.com'), Connected(url='ws://www.google.com'), Rejected(<response HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request>, 'Websocket upgrade failed (code=400)'), Disconnected('error; file descriptor cannot be a negative integer (-1)', graceful=False)]
error in websocket loop
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Projects\venv\websocket_client_playground\lib\site-packages\lomond\session.py", line 384, in run
    readable = selector.wait_readable(poll)
  File "C:\Projects\venv\websocket_client_playground\lib\site-packages\lomond\selectors.py", line 32, in wait_readable
    select.select([self._socket.fileno()], [], [], timeout)
ValueError: file descriptor cannot be a negative integer (-1)
```

Environment:
* Python 3.5.3
* lomond 0.2.1
* Windows 10 Pro 1709 16299.309

When a `HandshakeError` is encountered in `WebSocket.feed()`, leading to a `Rejected` event, the socket is ultimately closed.  The issue is with the call to `select()` in `SelectSelector.wait_readable()` which happens after this, as `self._socket.fileno()` will return -1.

A trivial solution is to wrap the code in `wait_readable()` in a blanket try/except and return `True` in the failure case.  This will allow normal processing to proceed in `WebsocketSession.run()` and the session will close out gracefully.

issue_title: custom css for all of admin
issue_body:
Hi,
I have been trying to customise the CSS for the admin site with varying success.

I have implemented custom AdminForms, using the Media class (as it happens actually overriding the media property for my purposes, but the Media class worked as well) to communicate my custom CSS to the forms. That works fine.

I also use a custom AdminSite and, likewise, define a Media class with my custom CSS. To this custom AdminSite, I register all my forms, plus a few models from the standard site (User, Group). However the non customised admin forms (User and Group) as well as the supporting pages, (index, applications, logout etc) ignore this custom CSS.

The solution I found was twofold. First in the file django_admin_bootstrapped/templates/admin/base.html I think there should be a `{{ media.css }}` inserted in the "extrastyle" block. This propagates quite nicely since most of the derived templates call `{{ block.super }}`. Second in the function `each_context` inside sites.py, one needs to add a key for the "media" attribute to the dict that is returned. I did not really want to patch Django, so for my purpose I just wrote an override function for this in my custom AdminSite.

With these two changes in place it works better. I am happy with overriding the `each_context` function. Is it sensible to put the media.css patch in to this project?

Cheers,
Philip

Actually just checking has shown that, login and confirm action (at least) are still broken even with my changes above... (more to check)

issue_title: Does not work on app engine anymore because on new parallel bulk
issue_body:
This [line](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/master/elasticsearch/helpers/__init__.py#L4) prevent it from working under app engine, where multiprocessing is not supported. Exception looks like:
```
 from multiprocessing.dummy import Pool
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/multiprocessing/__init__.py", line 65, in <module>
    from multiprocessing.util import SUBDEBUG, SUBWARNING
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/multiprocessing/util.py", line 41, in <module>
    from subprocess import _args_from_interpreter_flags
```

How do we can prevent multiprocessing module to being imported while running on app engine?
Its possible to do explicit check, like it is done in [NLTK](https://github.com/nltk/nltk/blob/develop/nltk/data.py#L770) or we could move import statement inside of parallel_bulk function, which is against PEP8 – but it will solve the problem.

issue_title: Out of memory when running az storage blob upload
issue_body:
I'm using az to upload an image to azure.
I'm using python3, but have seen the same issue with python2.
Image is 30G (although only 1,5 G sparse).
Eventually I managed to upload using --max-connections 1.
I traced the issue down to this little piece of code:
file: storage/blob/_upload_chunking.py, line:70
    if max_connections > 1:
        import concurrent.futures
        executor = concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_connections)
        range_ids = list(executor.map(uploader.process_chunk, uploader.get_chunk_streams()))
    else:
        range_ids = [uploader.process_chunk(result) for result in uploader.get_chunk_streams()]
Problem is with the line
        range_ids = list(executor.map(uploader.process_chunk, uploader.get_chunk_streams()))
calling executor.map with parameter uploader.get_chunk_streams() creates a list with all the element that are yielded in get_chunk_streams().
This list holds all 30G of file data and is built in memory before passing it on to executor.map().

So, if you want to upload with maxconnections > 1, basically, you need at least (memory+swap) larger than file you wish to upload...

issue_title: OSProvisioningTimedOut with IaaS VM Name because OS Disk is not unique
issue_body:
CRI 60564263

Getting an OSProvisioningTimedOut (OSPTO) with the following repro steps:
- create a VM named "XYZ" from a Marketplace Image
- delete the VM
- create a new VM with same name "XYZ" from Marketplace image
--> Provisioning failed. OS Provisioning for VM '1ce0l5gdqjuo0t242' did not finish in the allotted time. The VM may still finish provisioning successfully. Please check provisioning state later.. OSProvisioningTimedOut


Reason is that the following UserAgent "_python/3.5.1 (Linux-3.10.0-514.10.2.el7.x86_64-x86_64-with-redhat-7.2-Maipo) requests/2.14.2 msrest/0.4.10 msrest_azure/0.4.9 computemanagementclient/1.0.0 Azure-SDK-For-Python_" seems to give the VM's OS Disk name the same value as the VM Name ("XYZ" in our case).
So when you create a new VM from an image, Azure passes the VHD URL of the blob that already exists - and which is already specialized & provisioned.
Hence Azure tries to create a VM with CreateOption = FromImage and it passes a Specialized VHD.
--> OSProvisioningTimedOut.


ExecutionGraph has the following signature:
/etc/hostname contains 'XYZ' and not 'localhost.localdomain'. Also /var/lib/waagent/provisioned is already existing. As a result the Linux image was already specialized.

Ask is to give the OS Disk name a unique name, different than the VM Name...

issue_title: Feature request: a special "<text>" value for the hooks.yaml "files" field
issue_body:
Hi,

I had to implement this kind of "binary-files-filtering-out" today:
https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/pre_commit_hooks/utils.py#L10

So I made a quick overview of the existing checkers, and I found only 2 that are targeting **any** text file: [end-of-file-fixer](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/hooks.yaml#L76) and [trailing-whitespaces](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/hooks.yaml#L106). Both use a long list of known text files prefixes.

If ever you think this use case could come in handy, I'd suggest to provide a magic value like `<any text file>` to the `files` parameter in _hooks.yaml_, so that hooks developers don't have to re-implement this filtering each time.

You can freely re-use the code I mentioned above if it comes handy to you.

Also, sorry for the 3-issues-opened-in-a-row :) I got really excited by this tool and suggestions for improvements just flowed in ! They are really just humble features suggestions.

Cheers

I'm doing a `POST` request via `client.post`:

```
data = aiohttp.FormData({

        }, charset='windows-1251')

client.post(base_url, params={'RowFrom': offset}, data=data)

POST data should contain: `FindText=%D5%E0%ED`

`FindText=%D1%85%D0%B0%D0%BD'`

Looking through the code of `formdata.py:99`

urlencode(data, doseq=True).encode(charset),

```

issue_title: Built-in browser spell checker does not work in markdown cells
issue_body:
In web based tools I've come to expect that I can use the built-in browser spell checker to avoid spelling mistakes, however this does not work in Jupyter markdown cells. 

It appears the issue is markdown cells are CodeMirror input areas, which does not support browser spell checking.  Given that Markdown cells are text not code do they really need to be CodeMirror input areas? My assumption is CodeMirror provides little or no benefit for Markdown cells.

If Markdown cells could just use standard HTML Textarea this would instantly enable the built in browser spell checking and/or any other browser functionality that works on standard text input. 

A more complete solution would to create a pluggable architecture for cells that enables each cell type to have its  user configurable, pluggable javascript handler that is installed and configured using extensions. Jupyter would then ship with a standard:
* CodeMirror extension that creates a Code cells type that is  mapped to a CodeMirror handler 
* Markdown extension that creates a Markdown cell type that is mapped to a HTML Textarea

Upgrading CodeMirror is then as simple as upgrading and installing a new extension.

New extensions could be created to:
* Replace the standard Markdown extension with a lightweight Markdown editor that support editing mathematics, e.g., LaTex
* Create an HTML cell type that is mapped to an javascript HTML editor like TinyMCE or Ckeditor

issue_title: UX Bug: command palette uses confusing labeling
issue_body:
![screen shot 2017-03-07 at 12 33 45 pm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/185923/23674213/5a440796-0332-11e7-82fa-dbfe77f4de20.png)

I saw the label _(command)_, which is also the name of a key on a Mac keyboard, so I attempted to run a cell using `command+shift+enter`, which didn't do anything.  The correct command was just `shift+enter`.

A few possible solutions:

- remove the _(command)_ label.  What is it? Who cares about it?
- Move it away from the shortcuts. Maybe to the left side of the row?
- rename the _(command)_ label to _(commands)_
- use more words to clarify, e.g. _type:command_ or _(runs a command)_ or _(cell command)_
- use a synonym for _command_, e.g. _directive_ or _instruction_

issue_title: "Make a copy" menu item loses unsaved changes
issue_body:
Sometimes I find myself making changes to a notebook, then realise that I should keep my original notebook for comparison reasons.

So I use the "Make a copy" option to save a copy of the notebook with my changes (and go back to undo them from my original file). However, this make a copy of the current *notebook state on disk, neglecting any unsaved changes*.

I appreciate that this may be subjective, but this behaviour seems quite counter-intuitive to me. My expectation of this option is that it should operate more like "Save As...", so that the current contents of the notebook gets saved to a new filename.

issue_title: Casting from float to int for the purpose of indexing can break things
issue_body:
This is a bit of a silly example, but numpy indexing requires integers, however this ends up breaking autograd.

```
import numpy as np
import autograd.numpy as anp
from autograd import grad
def fun(W, inds):
   W = anp.concatenate((W, inds), axis=1)
   inds = W[:,-1]
   W = W[:,:-1]
   return W[anp.int64(inds)].sum()

W = np.random.randn(5, 10)
inds = np.ones(5)[:,None]
g = grad(fun)
g(W, inds)
```

produces `TypeError: long() argument must be a string or a number, not 'FloatNode'`

issue_title: Improvements to docker install instructions
issue_body: I want to run a full node on a ubuntu virtual machine. It's 16.0.4.3 LTS. I'm trying to follow the docker instructions on https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-core#install-with-docker My background is: I know what docker is. I recall installing it before as part of the discourse forum software install instructions. I've heard it makes setting up server side apps easier because you don't have to manage a bunch of dependencies. I don't use it regularly though. I'm intentionally following instructions as they are written even when I know they won't work. --- I downloaded the `launcher` script by clicking on "download" but it didn't download, it opened in my browser. I realized I probably need to copy and past the script text into a file on the server. I try to run `./launcher pull`. I get an error. Ahh I need to make the script executable. Instructions didn't tell me that. Another error. Hmm I don't have docker installed. The instructions didn't tell me to install docker or how I can do that. I try `apt-get install docker`. It installs something. yay! I have docker! Try to run `./launcher pull`. Hmm..the same error `docker` not found. I guess that wasn't docker? By now I'm lost. The next step is i have to go down a rabbit hole of figuring out what docker is (because apparently `apt-get install docker` wasn't it and how to install it. If i'm really motivated and have a lot of time, I'll go figure out how to install docker by googling around. But I came here trying to learn about blockstack, not docker. Our docker install instructions were supposed to make it faster and easier than the `pip install` instructions, but it didn't succeed in doing that. --- I think docker install for running full code nodes on servers has the potential to make this a lot easier for everyone. It would be great if we can review the instructions and make them super easy to follow for someone with basic linux command line ability. Discourse does this very well with their instructions for installing discourse in the cloud via docker. All you have to do is exactly follow the steps they give you and you get a discourse. My suggestion would be to approach our instructions in the same way. Some one should be able to follow them step by step without having to assume anything more than a general ability to use the unix command line. I really like how the discourse instructions tell you exactly what version of what operating system you should install. I don't think we need to go so far as push people to a particular cloud vendor, but telling them to start with a Ubuntu 16.04 LTS install might be a good idea so that everyone is on the same page.

issue_title: Unique Stacks address format
issue_body:
Moved from: https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-core/issues/822#issuecomment-391680586

I think from a product and user experience perspective it makes more sense to use different address formats for the different token types.

There are a number of reasons for this. One reason is that without its "own" format like ethereum has it's own 0x format, it doesn't seem like a full blown separate coin.

Another reason is to avoid user confusion. A bitcoin address is a bitcoin address. If i want to ask someone for the account to which they'd like to send me Stacks, do I ask them for a bitcoin address? How can someone looking at the string determine if the wallet software that generated supports stacks or only bitcoin?

issue_title: Add Activate operation support to the server
issue_body: The KMIP specification defines a basic state machine for managed objects that maps to their natural and security lifecycle. Several KMIP operations directly impact object state, allowing subsequent operations to occur in a fully featured server application. Add support for the `Activate` operation, which transitions managed objects from the `Pre-Active` state to the `Active` state, officially earmarking them as usable entities.

issue_title: encoding fails with non-BMP characters on narrow python builds
issue_body:
TIL: some python builds don't support non-BMP characters in their strings: http://wordaligned.org/articles/narrow-python

The symptoms of this are things like:

```
  File "/var/synapse/.synapse/lib/python2.7/site-packages/canonicaljson.py", line 142, in encode_canonical_json
    return _unascii(s)
  File "/var/synapse/.synapse/lib/python2.7/site-packages/canonicaljson.py", line 121, in _unascii
    chunks.append(unichr(c))
exceptions.ValueError: unichr() arg not in range(0x10000) (narrow Python build)
```

Since we're only using unichr here to build a string which we're about to utf-8 encode, we could instead add utf-16 delegates, which apparently are handled correctly by `.encode("utf-8")`. 

In fact, given `.encode("utf-8")` handles utf-16 delegates, why are we bothering to unpack the utf-16 delegates at all?

issue_title: Review all IOStore stuff
issue_body:
As far as I can tell, the inputstore (command line parameter given to all tools) serves no real use.  If this is indeed the case, it should be gotten rid of. 

The outputstore is currently being used to pass data between pipeline phases (index -> map -> call).  This makes sense, but may need to be made optional for performance.  Should think of abstracting this code so it can be toggled in one place.  

The tools also have an out_dir parameter, which seems redundant given that they write to the output store. 

Summary:
* Get rid of input stores
* Get rid of output dir
* Use output store only for writing final output.  But have option to checkpoint intermediate data in output store as done currently.

issue_title: Implement encryption key management service
issue_body:
The main purpose is to prevent us from having to distribute the master key to the operators within the core group, i.e. the people running the pipelines on controlled-access data. The master key should only be in the possession of a very limited number of people. But most importantly, you shouldn't have to pass the most important secret to give an operator access to the system. 

The solution should also be simple and low-tech.

Instead of GPG, I think we should piggy-back on SSH keys because everyone is bound to have one already. Here is the outline:

1) A user requests to be authorized by sending their public key to the key admin. The key admin is in possession of the master key.

2) The key admin generates a plaintext token (random string + user_id + expiration) using the service's public key and the users public key and sends the twice-encrypted token back to the user.

3) The key admin uses the plaintext token to encrypt the master key and deposits the encrypted master key on the machine running the service. This will be known as the user-specific master key.

4) The user sends a request to the key management service containing the token and a list of URLs. The token is the once-decrypted token, i.e. it is still encrypted with the service's public key.

5) Using its private key, the service decrypts the token into plain text, validates the token, loads the user-specific master key into memory and then uses the token to decrypt the user-specific master key. This will result in the clear-text master key in memory on the machine running the service. It then uses the plain-text master key to derive a per-file key for each of the submitted URLs and sends the result back. TBD: Should we use a nonce for the derivation?

The resulting per-URL keys could be encrypted using the once-encrypted token or we could rely the security of the underlying channel through which request and response are communicated, i.e. HTTPS. Or a combination of both.

TBD: Access control to the service machine.

issue_title: Need to call `asyncio.set_event_loop()` in my tests
issue_body:
Hi there!

I'm porting some of my tests from v0.3.0 to v0.5.0 and I have a test that behaves strangely when I switch to v0.4.1 or later.

After some debugging, I noticed that `id(event_loop)` inside the test and `id(asyncio.get_event_loop())` return different results for versions after v0.4.1 and v0.5.0.

After adding a call to `asyncio.set_event_loop(event_loop)` in my tests like this:

```python
def test_serve(event_loop, cli):
    asyncio.set_event_loop(event_loop)  # wasn't required with v0.3.0.
```

Notice that I'm not using `@pytest.mark.asyncio` here.  I can't use it because I'm testing a [click](http://click.pocoo.org/) CLI that calls `event_loop.run_until_complete()` internally.

I guess this is explained by the fact that the `event_loop` fixture stopped calling `asyncio.set_event_loop()` when the `@pytest.mark.asyncio(forbid_global_event_loop=...)` syntax was introduced in v0.4.0.

In short, it seems like using only the `event_loop` fixture (e.g. without `@pytest.mark.asyncio`) leads to a surprising result.  I thing that the (absence of a) relationship between the `event_loop` fixture and `asyncio.get_event_loop()` should be added to the documentation to make it more obvious.  If you're OK with this, I can send in a PR to fix it.

In passing, I think there's a big quirk in the API here: the behaviour for using the `event_loop` fixture without the `asyncio` marker is surprising, to say to least.  The havior in v0.3.0 was much better than it is now in that respect.  This seems to be caused by the fact that `forbid_global_event_loop` is marker parameter rather than a fixture.  If the policy for controlling the relationship with the default event loop were a fixture, it would allow the `event_loop` fixture to decide what to do with the default event loop.

I've seen @asvetlov's comments in issue #12 and PR #24.  There seems to be some contention over whether returning exceptions from `asyncio.get_event_loop()` should be the default or not.  I don't have a specific position on this, but I am convinced that either it should return the same value as the `event_loop` fixture *or* it should fail (having two event loops by default is not ideal).  A neat side-effect of making the policy for the default event loop a fixture on which the `event_loop` fixture depends (instead of a parameter to the marker) would be to allow defining a project-specific default: each project would be able to define this new fixture as something returning a boolean (or enum value) to choose the policy and use `autouse` to make it a project specific default.

What are your thoughts on this?

issue_title: Toooooo slow interacting with MS sql server under Ubuntu 16.04 (both using EF or SQLDataReader)
issue_body:
# Issue Title

Huge big time for loading data from SQL server (probably serialization or disconnecting)

# General

I have a web API application on dotnet under Core 2.0, Ubuntu 16.04, and an MS SQL server as data storage (totally < 1Gb of data there). Core i5 with 8 GB memory laptop, SSD 120 GB. Both the app and the server run on same machine (localhost).

When loading a table of about 150 000 rows (about 10 columns) from sql server I get about 300 sec time! Smaller tables also loaded in such a big time (proportionally to the number of rows). All requests are sync (async versions never used).

This happens for both the EF approach or with direct using of SQLDataReader, so the problem is NOT in the EF.

As a workaround I simply run sqlcmd tool with the sql request as a separate process and parse its output as a cvs file. This takes about 2 sec (not 300 sec) for the same tables.

My gues is that Core 2.0 handles the connection in some bad manner (eg. disconnects and connects on each row of table) or there's something in deserialization (though I think just any kind of deserialization woldn't be that bad) or maybe Core 2.0 switches to async methods under the hood (which in turn set a kind of timer and wakes on receiving each piece of data) or so on, but these are only my guesses.

Maybe it's somehow connected with the well known MS SQL server bud (feature?) under linux: sqlcmd becomes toooo slow when std output in redirected to a file by '>' in the command line (special option should be used instead). But maybe it has nothing to do with that. (Note: I don't redirect the output in my sqlcmd request which I use a workaround, just read it line by line as a process std output.) So maybe Core 2.0 works the same bad way with MS SQL server as sqlcmd does when redirected to file (switches to async mode? sets a timer and waits for it on each line of data?)

issue_title: Track dead/alive proxies with authentication
issue_body:
The built-in HttpProxyMiddleware will correctly set up the authentication parameters for proxies sent up from the rotating proxies plugin. However, by doing so, request.meta['proxy'] field is changed to only contain the raw proxy_url, as the credentials are ripped out. When the response is received, rotating proxies will try to mark the proxy as good or dead, but will silently fail because of the 'proxy not in self.proxies' check, resulting in all proxies staying unmarked forever. This behaviour can be verified by using any proxy with authentication and observing that the logstats keeps logging everything as unchecked while scrapy is crawling.

Easy fix is to update '_handle_result' to identify the proxy in self.proxies corresponding to the input request.meta['proxy'], and use this unabridged proxy in the rest of the call. Will make a PR for this unless you have any objections to this approach

issue_title: Make backoff cap configurable
issue_body:
The current backoff algorithm is quite effective, but I think it would benefit from supporting configuration of the 'cap' used for the time limit. It's very possible a cluster of proxies could go down for several days, but the fixed cap used at present ensures they'll be checked around once every half hour at minimum, which isn't terribly sensible in this use case.

The fix would be to simply support the ROTATING_PROXY_BACKOFF_CAP setting, which would be used in the same way as the existing ROTATING_PROXY_BACKOFF_BASE to initialize the backoff function.

issue_title: KafkaConsumer and current positions
issue_body:
When the consumer starts and there is no new messages for the group it is impossible to know current positions and to commit current offsets.

Committing offsets even if there are no new messages can be important because offsets in Kafka have an expiration time. So to prevent offsets from expiring we need to commit them periodically, even is there are no changes.

Normal `commit()` doesn't help because it won't do anything and would return `KafkaError.NO_OFFSETS`.

It is possible to use `commit(offsets)` and give it specific offsets, but how to get them?

Currently even if the consumer gets partitions assigned, `consumer.assignment()` returns an empty list. I strongly suspect that even if I receive messages it will only give me partitions for the received messages, not all of them.

OK, I can get a list of assigned partitions from `on_assign` callback. But then when I call `position(_assigned)` it won't give me current offsets for the group, only for the partitions I received messages from.

Adding the ability to query currently assigned offsets would be very helpful.
Making `commit()` committing offsets even if nothing changed would be even more helpful.

issue_title: inconsistant behavious of parse parameter dayfirst
issue_body:
I realized a change from version 2.5.1 to 2.5.2 in regards to ``parse`` and the parameter ``datefirst``. In my application I have crappy legacy dates in the format: * 2017/04/01 * 01/04/2017 Both meaning April 1st, 2017. I am using this code: 
from dateutil.parser import parse

ts = "01/04/2017"
d = parse(ts, dayfirst=True)
print("{0!r}".format(d))

ts = "2017/04/01"
d = parse(ts, dayfirst=True)
print("{0!r}".format(d))
From 2.4.2 to 2.5.1 this code will produce April 1st in both cases. Starting with 2.5.2 it will create January 4th in the second case. It looks like, the parameter ``dayfirst`` was ignored in version <=2.5.1 when parsing an American style date "2017/04/01" but is applied in newer version >=2.5.2. Is this the intended behaviour? Is there a recommendation for dealing with this change from version 2.5.1 to 2.5.2?

issue_title: feature request: FILETIME support and milisecond epoch time support
issue_body:
Hello,

First, thanks for `dateutil`, it is by far the best package I've ever used for parsing dates "the right way" without doing much thinking. I often deal with large datasets from third party APIs who take liberties with standard formats, and it is terrific for saving me time.

With that said, it would be great if `dateutil` could recognize and `parse` Microsoft FILETIME format, which are most commonly represented by 64-bit integers. This date format is seen commonly in some Active Directory objects (both user and computer objects have some attributes in FILETIME) and is used as a timestamp on files, apparently (I don't work with Windows much, believe it or not)

FILETIME is a proprietary 64-bit number constructed using milliseconds and based on a special (non-UNIX) epoch- 1601 instead of 1/1/1970. Microsoft describes it as "Contains a 64-bit value representing the number of 100-nanosecond intervals since January 1, 1601 (UTC)."

So basically, annoying, but used a lot if you're developing in an AD environment. It's not a difficult implementation, but it would be really nice to have this functionality in dateutil

There is an example 3 line implementation (here)[https://gist.github.com/Mostafa-Hamdy-Elgiar/9714475f1b3bc224ea063af81566d873] though I can't comment on how robust it is. Because the number involved are 64-bit it might be wiser to implement using numy or similar. For convenience, there's a trivial converter available [here](http://www.silisoftware.com/tools/date.php?inputdate=128271382742968750&inputformat=filetime) for testing or investigating

For those of you that come from a C background, [this](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/help/188768/info-working-with-the-filetime-structure) is much easier to understand 

As an aside: I'm not sure that you wants to get into the business of interpreting "special" values in dates, but in my implementation, I handle the special value `2^63-1` as a None value

I would be happy to add my implementation (after switching to proper strong datatypes) if it is something the project would be interested in.

Also, I'm sorry to mix two issues, but they are kind of along the same lines. MSFT AD also likes to store "milisecond epoch" time- basically UNIX epoch time, but as miliseconds since 1/1/1970. I have a simple implementation for this (that I would want to clean up after looking through the standards of the project) and also would like if it would be considered for inclusion.

If this is a strict no, I'd like to know the reason, I admittedly did not read much about the project aside from the contributing guidelines, so apologies if this is not proper protocol for feature requests.

Thanks again, let me know and if this is something desired, I'll bang out the PRs once I get a feel for the state machine in the dateutil parser (I think I recall seeing a proper state machine somewhere in there...)

issue_title: Feature request: handling known failure testing
issue_body:
Sometimes we need to test that the workflow engine fails its execution with certain inputs as expected.

For example, [section 11 in user guide](http://www.commonwl.org/user_guide/11-records/) shows an example CWL file and a job file that will fail its execution.
Another example is the conformance test for workflow engines for CWL. The specification says that the execution should fail its execution in some cases.

It will be nice if `cwltest` can handle this kind of tests.

Related: [Issue #37 in user_guide](https://github.com/common-workflow-language/user_guide/issues/37)

issue_title: dump_only attributes passed in Meta options are not marked as readOnly
issue_body:
Reported by @lafrech in https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/pull/81#issuecomment-233417809:

---

I noticed this only adds readOnly if the field was provided dump_only attribute directly. If the field appears in the dump_only list in Meta, readOnly is not added.

Is this known/intended?

More precisely, it "does not work" if spec.definition is passed a marshmallow.schema.SchemaMeta but it "works" if it is passed a Schema instance:

class GistSchema(ma.Schema):
    id = ma.fields.Int()
    content = ma.fields.Str()

```
class Meta:
    dump_only = ('content',)
```
# readOnly not added

spec.definition('Gist', schema=GistSchema)
# readOnly added

spec.definition('Gist', schema=GistSchema())
I just begun experimenting with apispec and I'm not sure passing an instance is the right thing to do. In the docs, a SchemaMeta is passed.

issue_title: Allow strict=False to override strict setting in Meta options class.
issue_body:
I prefer ```strict=True``` by default (as someone else requested in another issue), so I was setting it in the Meta class to True... but then I found I could not set it to False in cases when I wanted it to be False.

Workaround for now... implemented a BaseSchema with overridden strict kwarg like this:

```python
class BaseSchema(Schema):
    def __init__(self, strict=True, **kwargs):
        super(BaseSchema, self).__init__(strict=strict, **kwargs)
```

issue_title: In strict mode, raise UnmarshallingError with full errors dictionary
issue_body:
Currently, strict mode will cause an error to be raised as soon as validation fails on a field. The error contains information about the single field.

It would be more useful to validate the entire input dictionary and raise an error with the full errors dictionary.

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields

class MySchema(Schema):
    email = fields.Email()
    homepage = fields.URL()
    
    class Meta:
        strict = True
    
s = MySchema()

# Current
s.validate({'email': 'invalid', 
            'homepage': 'not-a-url'})  # UnmarshallingError: ['"not-a-url" is not a valid URL.']

# Proposed
s.validate({'email': 'invalid', 
            'homepage': 'not-a-url'})  # UnmarshallingError: {'email': ['"invalid" is not a valid email.'],
                                       #                      'homepage': ['"not-a-url" is not a valid URL.']}
```

issue_title: Provide end-user readable string in ValidationError
issue_body:
The default string representation of ValidationError shows a Python dictionary structure, which may be hard to understand by non-programmers - for example: 

`{'field1': ['Missing data for required field.'], 'field2': ['Invalid data.']}`

I suggest to provide a `ValidationError.list()` method which returns a list of messages in the form:

```
['field1: Missing data for required field',
'field2: Invalid data',
'field3.nested1: Invalid data']
```

which can be `'; '.join(messages)` easily into a string or returned as bullets at the convenience of the caller. 

It is important that the nesting is handled properly; i.e., flattened using a delimiter. 

Thanks again and best regards,
Martin

issue_title: rfc2217.Serial() initialization broken
issue_body:
When one initializes rfc2217.Serial() with a port, then all the attributes initialized by the parent method here https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/master/serial/rfc2217.py#L383 will be overwritten with "None" starting with this line: https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/master/serial/rfc2217.py#L384

The typical error one will get because of this is as follows (when calling write()):

`  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/serial/rfc2217.py", line 628, in write
    with self._write_lock:
AttributeError: __exit__`

Looks like this was broken starting in February this year because of PyLint comments... To fix this, all this None initializations of the attributes need to happen *before* calling super() - this way the correctly set ones won't get overwritten...

WORKAROUND:
-call rfc2217.Serial() without the port parameter and afterwards call open() on the returned object.

issue_title: tools not loaded for Gromacs 2016/2018 in Python 3
issue_body:
When using Gromacs 2016/2018 (the `gmx` command) and Python 3 (and apparently only on macOS) then the tools are not correctly loaded; only `tools.Gmx` appears.

Reason: byte string snafu

```python
            out = subprocess.check_output([driver, '-quiet', 'help',
                                           'commands'])
            for line in str(out).encode('ascii').splitlines()[5:-1]:
```
The `subprocess.check_output()` returns a `b` string, 
```python
>>> type(out)
bytes
```
which, when converted with `str()`, does not maintain the newlines but rather includes them quoted:
```python
>>> str(b"\n")
"b'\\n'"
```
and then `splitlines()` does not work anymore.

issue_title: Parametrized factory (reusable factory)
issue_body:
I have different Service Objects which have a Database object injected in order to abstract from the ORM (better testability). 
The default implementation is an SqlAlchemy implementation, which has 2 parameters, a base_class and a session.

I have these two factories:

    tokens = providers.Factory(services.TokenService,
                               id_generator=id_generator,
                               database=providers.Factory(
                                   services.SqlAlchemyDatabaseService,
                                   session=session,
                                   base_class=models.Token)
                               )

    users = providers.Factory(services.UserService,
                               id_generator=id_generator,
                               database=providers.Factory(
                                   services.SqlAlchemyDatabaseService,
                                   session=session,
                                   base_class=models.User)
                               )

As you can see, the ```database``` factory should be moved away, so the parameters that are the same for all usages are in only one place. Ideally, I would like to have something like:

    database = providers.Factory(services.SqlAlchemyDatabaseService,
                                 session=session)

    tokens = providers.Factory(services.TokenService,
                               id_generator=id_generator,
                               database=database.extend(base_class=models.Token)

So database lacks one parameter, which could be provided by some decorator like that hypothetical ```extend()``` method. Is it something that is already implemented?

issue_title: Allow to include content from external file in template
issue_body:
Feature Request: Allow to include content from external file

The UserData parts of a template can get pretty big.
It would be nice to be able to include those parts from separate files.
Having them in separate files would also allow to syntax-check them in code editors.

Example: Instead of having part of a senza template file look like:
```
...
Resources:
  InstanceController
    Type: AWS::EC2::Instance
    Properties:
      AvailabilityZone: eu-west-1a
      BlockDeviceMappings:
        - DeviceName: "/dev/xvda"
          Ebs:
            VolumeSize: '30'
            VolumeType: gp2
      IamInstanceProfile:
        Ref: IAMInstanceProfileController
      ImageId: ami-a94c02da
      InstanceType: m3.medium
      NetworkInterfaces:
        - AssociatePublicIpAddress: false
          DeleteOnTermination: true
      Tags:
        - Key: KubernetesCluster
          Value: kubernetes-kube-aws
        - Key: Name
          Value: kubernetes-kube-aws-kube-aws-controller
      UserData:
        Fn::Base64:
          |
            #cloud-config <---- this part can be long/complex. It would be better in a separate file
            coreos:
              update:
                reboot-strategy: "off"
              flannel:
                interface: $private_ipv4
                etcd_endpoints: http://10.0.40.50:2379
              etcd2:
                name: controller
                advertise-client-urls: http://$private_ipv4:2379
                initial-advertise-peer-urls: http://$private_ipv4:2380
                listen-client-urls: http://0.0.0.0:2379
                listen-peer-urls: http://0.0.0.0:2380
                initial-cluster: controller=http://$private_ipv4:2380
              units:
                - name: etcd2.service
            # ...
            # (loooong cloud-config)  
            # ...
```

We could replace the userdata content by something like ` {{ SenzaReplace: ./cloudinit.yaml }}`
It could look like this:
```
...
Resources:
  InstanceController
    Type: AWS::EC2::Instance
    Properties:
      AvailabilityZone: eu-west-1a
      BlockDeviceMappings:
        - DeviceName: "/dev/xvda"
          Ebs:
            VolumeSize: '30'
            VolumeType: gp2
      IamInstanceProfile:
        Ref: IAMInstanceProfileController
      ImageId: ami-a94c02da
      InstanceType: m3.medium
      NetworkInterfaces:
        - AssociatePublicIpAddress: false
          DeleteOnTermination: true
      Tags:
        - Key: KubernetesCluster
          Value: kubernetes-kube-aws
        - Key: Name
          Value: kubernetes-kube-aws-kube-aws-controller
      UserData:
        Fn::Base64:
          |
            {{ SenzaReplace: ./cloudinit.yaml }}
```
and file `cloudinit.yaml` would then have the content:
```
#cloud-config
coreos:
  update:
    reboot-strategy: "off"
  flannel:
    interface: $private_ipv4
    etcd_endpoints: http://10.0.40.50:2379
  etcd2:
    name: controller
    advertise-client-urls: http://$private_ipv4:2379
    initial-advertise-peer-urls: http://$private_ipv4:2380
    listen-client-urls: http://0.0.0.0:2379
    listen-peer-urls: http://0.0.0.0:2380
    initial-cluster: controller=http://$private_ipv4:2380
  units:
    - name: etcd2.service
# ...
# (loooong cloud-config)  
# ...
```
That content could be yaml/bash/sh... and could syntax-checked (in a code editor or a validation tool during continuous integration).
Senza would then replace {{ SenzaReplace: ./cloudinit.yaml }} by the content of cloudinit.yaml.

### Alternative 
Another way could be to replace:
```
UserData:
  Fn::Base64:
    |
      {{ SenzaReplace: ./cloudinit.yaml }}
```
By simply:
```
UserData: {{ SenzaUserData: ./cloudinit.yaml }}
```
Senza would then replace {{ SenzaUserData: ./cloudinit.yaml }} by the equivalent of:
`cat cloudinit.yaml | base64 | gunzip` to save space (the maximum size of a Cloudformation template is 51,200 bytes and UserData can occupy a lot of space).

issue_title: z3 solver's solution space of incremental solving
BV2Int Overflow Problems?
issue_body:
So I've been looking at converting a BitVector object (with it's own constraints already), into an Integer object. However, there appears to be strangeness with the `BV2Int` method.

Example:
```python
In [39]:  x = BitVec('x', 3)

In [40]: x
Out[40]: x

In [41]:  prove(x <= 3)
proved

In [42]:  prove(BV2Int(x) <= 3)
counterexample
[x = 6]
```

My guess is z3 is allowing overflow in handling of BV2Int object, thus 6 would indeed be possible, however undesirable behavior in my case.

Is there a way to make it honor over/underflows?

issue_title: Regression: detecting of unbounded objective
Z3Exception: Overflow encountered when expanding vector
RepeatLayer
When using standardize, scalses doesn't get returned as a parameter
issue_body:
```Python
In [1]: net = lasagne.layers.standardize(
   ...:     lasagne.layers.InputLayer((10, 20)), np.zeros(20), np.ones(20), 0)

In [2]: lasagne.layers.get_all_params(net)
Out[2]: [b]

In [3]: net.params
Out[3]: OrderedDict([(Elemwise{inv,no_inplace}.0, set())])

In [4]: net.get_params()
Out[4]: []

In [5]: theano.printing.debugprint(net.scales)
Elemwise{inv,no_inplace} [id A] ''
 |TensorConstant{(20,) of 1.0} [id B]
```

Maybe because it's using [`T.inv` on a `np.ndarray`](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/special.py#L232) (which gets converted to a `TensorConstant`, not a `SharedVariable`), so `collect_shared_vars` misses it?  I can't think of why someone would not want `scales` to be considered a parameter or to actually be a non-shared Theano variable, so I think requiring the user to pass in a shared variable or a `np.ndarray` is reasonable, and then just use `1./scale` instead of `T.inv(scale)`.

issue_title: saving with "to_X()" methods on NWBFile
issue_body:
idea for discussion:

I really like the way that pandas handles saving & converting data to various formats. Basically, any target format, whether that's saving to disk or converting to another python data format, uses the same syntax: methods on the object of the format "to_X" where X is the target format.

```
nwb_file.to_hdf('my_data.nwb')
```

this simplifies file saving for the user... they don't need to understand anything about `form` at all. further, they can readily discover supported target formats with simple tab completion.

obviously, behind the scenes this would be a simple wrapper for form's HDF5IO

similarly, one could imagine e.g. converting to an xarray with syntax like

```
xr_dataset = nwb_file.to_xarray()
```

issue_title: Docs about updating or adding data
issue_body:
I'm looking at pynwb and how I can use it in our pipeline, but it's not exactly clear to me some of the data manipulation aspects as a user:

* Can I make use of the fact that we're dealing with hdf5 files and directly manipulate the file, or do I always have to go through the pywnb wrapper.
* Specifically, the docs show how to create a file and add data, but how do I stream live data to the file? E.g. say I create a TimeSeries representing ephys data, how can keep appending data (efficiently) to the TimeSeries? E.g. h5py lets you do it with http://docs.h5py.org/en/latest/faq.html#appending-data-to-a-dataset.
* Say I have the file and then call `io.write(f)`. Then I add more data and call it again. Would it re-write the whole file again? What about huge files? I guess it isn't clear to me how thin the pynwb wrapper is on top of h5py or if it's completely seperate.
* It doesn't let me e.g. change the description of `ElectricalSeries` etc. once created. In a typical experiment I'd creates a bunch of data, write in a notepad the conditions for each run and then later fill in all the info to the file. But if it's write once that wouldn't work. I suppose I can create the data live and then later import it to a new file and add all this info then. But that may create data duplication, which is probably unwanted. Although I suppose it may be best to not edit original data at all once created, but rather link to it.

Thanks in advance!

issue_title: Releasing Packages Again
issue_body:
We're in a bit of a midway transition here on some packages, namely that we've bumped `react`, `react-dom`, some related packages, and `jest`. We need to bring up `flow` and `immutable` at some point, they seem a bit tough (read: time consuming), so I'm tempted to just get these out.

The other thing I put us on the hook for is supporting the vdom transform and `object-to-react` for jupyterlab + classic notebook. Jupyterlab is easy because they use commonjs -- the only extra we should provide are typescript definitions. For notebook, I'll figure out how to produce AMD and publish to bower.

Is there anything else you can think of (@lgeiger, @BenRussert?) that you'd want to see finished for when we release the next wave of packages that Hydrogen will end up with? I'm tempted to make many of these major bumps because of the change of `React` underneath to the next major version -- we can probably cause breakage otherwise if not everything gets upgraded together.

issue_title: Function prologue misplaced in CFG
issue_body:
Hi

While using `CFGFast` on a stripped openssl elf binary, i noticed that the results misplaced the `main` functions boundaries

The binary uses `__libc_start_main_ptr` to invoke it, as usual. 
The pointer passed to that correctly specifies the main to be at `0x430AF0`, IDA also finds it there.
But angr seems to think that the function starts `0xA` bytes after that. 
```
cfg = p.analyses.CFGFast()
print filter(lambda a: a >= 0x430AD0 and a <= 0x430C00, cfg.kb.functions.keys())                                                                        
[0x430afa]
```
IDA: Disassembly of the main.
```
.text:0000000000430AF0 ; int __cdecl main(int, char **, char **)
.text:0000000000430AF0 main            proc near           
.text:0000000000430AF0
.text:0000000000430AF0 ; __unwind {
.text:0000000000430AF0                 push    rbp
.text:0000000000430AF1                 push    r15
.text:0000000000430AF3                 push    r14
.text:0000000000430AF5                 push    r13
.text:0000000000430AF7                 push    r12
.text:0000000000430AF9                 push    rbx
.text:0000000000430AFA                 sub     rsp, 438h
[...]
```

Is this because `CFGFast` is searching for `sub rsp, $Frame` to detect function beginnings? I've found this pattern in other functions as well. 
@ltfish

Objects being wiped as soon as they're written
issue_body:
I've hit upon a strange bug in the new integration tests. The situation is that I want to add a job to a `JobTree` and then check to see if was added.

In the integration test however, the code seems to be adding the job to the tree and then making it as dirty which prompts a flush to disk. In the process of flushing to disk (using `VStreamer._raw_to_file`) it calls `GangaObject.accept` which requests read access (via `GangaObject._getReadAccess`) which calls `Registry.__safe_read_access` which evaluates `if this_id not in self._loaded_ids` to `True` and so calls `Registry.load` which loads stale information from disk, overwiting the fact that a job was just added.

The registry is question is the one found via `jobtree._impl._getRegistry()` which is the same `getRegistry('jobs').metadata`.

Now, this is only seen in the integration test and so there might just be a slight misconfiguration there but it is 100% reproducible. Have we seen this sort of behaviour before or does anyone (@rob-c) have an idea of what might be causing it?

issue_title: Splitting condor jobs by dataset in a single ganga job
issue_body:
Hi again,

I have a feature request that has come to me further down the analysis foodchain. I want to start a discussion here about how best to solve it. I'll start with outline:

We need a way to submit a job to run on a collection of datasets, and have the outputs preserve dataset boundaries so that no output file contains information from more than one dataset. 

When using the grid as a batch submission system, our submitter script (pathena) lets us create a task specifying as input a list of datasets in a text file (--inDsTxt="myfile.txt"), and with a couple of other options we can ensure that no job in the task will run over multiple datasets, and also we can ask for a part of the dataset name to be put into the output file name. So when running on dataset: mc15_13TeV.123456.something.something   we can ensure the '123456' appears in the name of the outputfile we produce, so it's easy to sort out which files came from which datasets.

Users are asking for something similar from condor batch submissions done with ganga. I am open to suggestions of how this would be achieved. We need a way to specify a list of datasets, where each dataset is itself a list of files (potential for wildcard use would be desirable here, for those of us who have organized our datasets into directories already). We'd like to do this submission as one ganga job (it is easier to bookkeep a single ganga job than multiple ones). The defined subjobs would need to preserve separation between the dataset files, and also provide a way to rename the created output file to contain (a part of) the dataset name. 

I appreciate this is definitely a feature request that could take some time. But I think it's something that needs to be developed for the way people have invested in their analysis setup downstream of the batch processing (I am not one of them, but sadly the rest of the collaboration appears to be).

Let me know if I can do anything to help
Thanks
Will

But [_change_response_class](https://github.com/scrapy-plugins/scrapy-splash/blob/0e3284abdcc2b4e1b228754f3c8ec76cceac4ec0/scrapy_splash/middleware.py#L394) in the splash middleware looks at the content type, so if it's binary, but response was a ``TextResponse``, the following error occurs:

issue_title: Repeated filter parameters
issue_body:
In the docs [https://pywb.readthedocs.io/en/develop/manual/cdxserver_api.html#filter](https://pywb.readthedocs.io/en/develop/manual/cdxserver_api.html#filter) it mentions that the filter parameter can be specified repeatedly. 
However after looking at the code the multiple filter parameters get compressed to just the last filter specified at `pywb/pywb/warcserver/basewarcserver.py` at line 63.
Is there currently a way to specify multiple filters?
