issue_title: Add ability to fiddle with services Pulp depends on
issue_body:
Let's add some mechanism(s) that let us fiddle with the services that Pulp depends on. Pulp is designed to be resilient against failure of certain services, and it would be nice if we could start or stop services at run-time to test that resilience.

At its simplest, the mechanism would look like this:

```python
def twiddle_service(state, services):
    # determine if remote system has `systemctl` available, or some other service manager
    # ssh some_user@remote_system systemctl state ' '.join(services)
```

One design consideration is what to do if the service you'd like to test might go by multiple names. For example, the message broker might be either RabbitMQ or Qpid. Do we make the user explicitly name the service to be twiddled, or do we provide abstraction? Another question is what to do if a non-zero return code is received. Do we raise an exception? (No, really. systemctl has a range of valid non-zero return codes.)

issue_title: Support Pulp 2.7+ in user-related test
issue_body:
Method `test_use_deleted_user` in class `pulp_smash.tests.platform.api_v2.test_user.ReadUpdateDeleteTestCase` isn't compatible with Pulp 2.7 and beyond. As of Pulp 2.7, calls to endpoints that require JSON but do not have a request body fail with a 400. This test fails because the PUT request requires a request body.

This should be addressed after #29.

(Thanks @asmacdo!)

issue_title: Deduplication
issue_body:
I was thinking in ways we could make [this #782](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/issues/782) work . I've also poked @syrusakbary on [twitter](https://twitter.com/syrusakbary/status/965660876333182976)...

The propose I was thinking about would improve the `middleware` class, adding a function just like Django's [new-style middleware](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/topics/http/middleware/#upgrading-pre-django-1-10-style-middleware).

We would have something like this:
```python
class AuthorizationMiddleware(object):
    def resolve(self, next_mw, root, info, **args):
        ## resolver (request) processing here

    def process_response(self, next_mw, response, **args):
        ## result (response) processing here
```

Does it make any sense?

issue_title: Universal sentence encoder retraining
issue_body:
Hi, 
I am trying to fine-tune the latest ("https://tfhub.dev/google/universal-sentence-encoder-large/3") USE on custom data. I want to set the learning rate low so that it does not significantly alter the original embeddings. So far I am a little confused how to do this.
So far I have looked at:
1/ https://www.tensorflow.org/hub/fine_tuning - to better understand fine-tuning and this was helpful
2/ I looked at #46 and #36 and these also contained some helpful info such as confirming that some form of retraining should be possible (i.e. a "fuzzy yes"). 
3/ I looked through the example code to use a classifier on top of the USE which shows the benefits of this form of transfer learning
4/ I have also looked through the general tf.hub docs to better understand how to use the feature columns and input functions when retaining pre-trained module.

But I am still unsure exactly how to implement this. For example, I was looking through the [USE](https://www.tensorflow.org/hub/modules/google/universal-sentence-encoder-large/3) docs to see if there were specs to show what is available for retraining. Does anyone have a simple code example of how this might look or maybe there are some docs I am missing to show how you would add your own layers ontop of the saved model with your own data?

Cheers
Cathal

issue_title: Certification: Joiner UDP port feature
issue_body:
Test Harness requires an API to configure **Joiner UDP Port** on commissioner side, discovery response message sent by commissioner needs to include a **Joiner UDP port TLV**, and the port should be used as destination port for UDP datagrams from joiner to commissioner.

We can still hardcode the UDP port to pass the test, but we need to support this feature eventually.

issue_title: get_network_driver is not backwards compatible
issue_body:
`get_network_driver` is currently looking for the `driver` class under `napalm.<driver>`, while in the past it was searching under `napalm_<driver>`. Not only this breaks backwards compatibility, but also napalm cannot actually be extended with community drivers. 
In the current form, the developer would need to create the following tree structure: `napalm/<driver>/<driver>.py`. As napalm is a dependency of the community driver, say `napalm-driver`, when executing `pip install napalm-driver` (which firstly install napalm and its structure), would override the `napalm` dir.
So this import doesn't actually work.
I'm wondering if we should instead rename the dirs from `napalm/<driver>` to `napalm_<driver>` which simplifies everything.
Thoughts @dbarrosop @ktbyers @bewing?

issue_title: Robustecer interpretación de listas a partir de strings separados por comas, en el reader de XLSX
issue_body:
**Contexto**

La lectura de catálogos en XLSX se basa en distintas estrategias para leer aquellos campos que son listas (en el XLSX se representan como strings).

En el caso de que la lista se lea como "valores separados por comas" a veces los usuarios ponen una coma de más, generando un último string vacío "" como valor en la lista.

**Propuesta**

Modificar la lectura de XLSX para que cuando parsea un string de valores separados por comas hacia una lista ignore los valores vacíos, evitando listas con valores vacíos.

**Notas**

Recordar hacer un nuevo micro release de pydatajson con esta modificación y actualizar la versión en monitoreo-apertura.

issue_title: API objects should accept an optional 'client_config' argument
issue_body:
Callers who want to override the defaults for an API instance should be able to pass in a dictionary containing those values:  if not passed, the constructor can then fall back to loading the JSON string from the file in the same directory.   E.g.:

```python
    def __init__(self,
                 service_path=SERVICE_ADDRESS,
                 port=DEFAULT_SERVICE_PORT,
                 channel=None,
                 ssl_creds=None,
                 scopes=None,
                 client_config=None,
                 retrying_override=None,
                 bundling_override=None,
                 timeout=_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT,
                 app_name='gax',
                 app_version=_GAX_VERSION):
        """Constructor.

        Args:
          service_path (string): The domain name of the API remote host.
          port (int): The port on which to connect to the remote host.
          channel (:class:`grpc.beta.implementations.Channel`): A ``Channel``
            object through which to make calls.
          ssl_creds (:class:`grpc.beta.implementations.ClientCredentials`):
            A `ClientCredentials` for use with an SSL-enabled channel.
          client_config (dict)
            dictionary that overrides default client configuration
          retrying_override (dict[string, :class:`google.gax.RetryOptions`]): A
            dictionary that overrides default retrying settings.
            ``retrying_override`` maps method names (e.g., ``'list_foo'``) to
            custom RetryOptions objects, or to None. A value of None indicates
            that the method in question should not retry.
          bundling_override (dict[string, :class:`google.gax.BundleOptions`]): A
            dictionary that overrides default bundling settings.
            ``bundling_override`` maps bundling method names (e.g.,
            'publish_foo') to custom BundleOptions objects, or to None. It is
            invalid to have a key for a method that is not bundling-enabled. A
            value of None indicates that the method in question should not
            bundle.
          timeout (int): The default timeout, in seconds, for calls made
            through this client
          app_name (string): The codename of the calling service.
          app_version (string): The version of the calling service.

        Returns:
          A PublisherApi object.
        """
        if scopes is None:
            scopes = self._ALL_SCOPES
        bundling_override = bundling_override or {}
        retrying_override = retrying_override or {}
        if client_config is None:
            client_config_json = pkg_resources.resource_string(
                __name__, 'publisher_client_config.json')
            client_config = json.loads(client_config_json)
        self._defaults = api_callable.construct_settings(
            'google.pubsub.v1.Publisher',
            client_config,
            bundling_override,
            retrying_override,
            config.STATUS_CODE_NAMES,
            timeout,
            bundle_descriptors=self._BUNDLE_DESCRIPTORS,
            page_descriptors=self._PAGE_DESCRIPTORS)
        google_apis_agent = '{}/{};{};gax/{};python/{}'.format(
            app_name, app_version, self._CODE_GEN_NAME_VERSION,
            self._GAX_VERSION, platform.python_version())
        self._headers = [('x-google-apis-agent', google_apis_agent)]
        self.stub = config.create_stub(pubsub_pb2.beta_create_Publisher_stub,
                                       service_path,
                                       port,
                                       ssl_creds=ssl_creds,
                                       channel=channel,
                                       scopes=scopes)

```

issue_title: Round-trip GeoJSON
issue_body:
I would like to be able to round-trip GeoJSON through csvkit, so that small operations can be done using csvkit in the middle, without resulting in strange diffs.  https://github.com/wireservice/csvkit/pull/867 is part of this effort.

i.e.  The following should have very minimal output.
```
$ wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/lyzidiamond/learn-geojson/master/geojson/pdxplaces.geojson
$ in2csv -f geojson pdxplaces.geojson > pdxplaces.csv
$ csvjson --lon longitude --lat latitude --indent=2 pdxplaces.csv > pdxplaces.csv.geojson
$ diff -u pdxplaces.geojson pdxplaces.csv.geojson
```

Some of the diff results which need to be controllable using command line args:

1. sequence of keys in a feature ; i.e. should `geometry` appear before `properties` or the opposite.  It seems different tools make different choices for this ordering, and ideally `in2csv` can annotate its output with this ordering (assuming it is consistent throughout the input) so that csvjson can re-use the same ordering.
2. do not emit empty properties.  Currently they are being emitted and mostly they shouldn't be emitted. (https://github.com/wireservice/csvkit/pull/869)
3. order the properties by key. `in2csv` adds a column when it sees a new property, which doesnt work well if many properties do not exist on the first `Feature`, but appear in latter nodes.  Sorting could be a feature of in2csv or `csvjson`
4. generation of the `bbox`.  It should be possible to disable this being computed and added, and it is non-trivial to compute it for complex geojson (c.f. https://github.com/wireservice/csvkit/pull/867).
5. other metadata (https://github.com/wireservice/csvkit/issues/870)

After simplistic solutions for those three, the diff looks like:

```diff
diff -u pdxplaces.geojson pdxplaces.csv.geojson
--- pdxplaces.geojson	2017-07-24 10:22:24.234446535 +0700
+++ pdxplaces.csv.geojson	2017-07-24 12:58:46.408943761 +0700
@@ -106,7 +106,7 @@
       "properties": {
         "Name": "place 2",
         "Contributor": "geografa",
-        "Reason": 2
+        "Reason": "2"
       },
       "geometry": {
         "type": "Point",
@@ -164,8 +164,7 @@
     {
       "type": "Feature",
       "properties": {
-        "my polygon": "it's here",
-        "Name": ""
+        "my polygon": "it's here"
       },
       "geometry": {
         "type": "Polygon",
@@ -251,8 +250,8 @@
       "type": "Feature",
       "properties": {
         "Name": "The Commons Brewery",
-        "Reason": "Farmhouse ales, duh.",
-        "Contributor": "Dillon Mahmoudi"
+        "Contributor": "Dillon Mahmoudi",
+        "Reason": "Farmhouse ales, duh."
       },
       "geometry": {
         "type": "Point",
@@ -267,7 +266,7 @@
       "properties": {
         "Name": "Kenilworth Coffeehouse",
         "Utility": "Excellent biscuits",
-        "Coffee": "Yes"
+        "Coffee": true
       },
       "geometry": {
         "type": "Point",
@@ -327,8 +326,7 @@
       "properties": {
         "Name": "Square 54",
         "Contributor": "Henrik",
-        "Reason": "Mystic zombie area",
-        "my polygon": ""
+        "Reason": "Mystic zombie area"
       },
       "geometry": {
         "type": "Polygon",
```

The change to `"Coffee"` is concerning (and maybe there is a command line arg which would prevent that), but the rest of those changes are IMO a good "linted" output.

issue_title: Table generation from CSV file failed
issue_body:
I'm trying to generate a table from a CSV file worked in previous version (0.9.1) but failed after upgrading to 1.0.2:

`head -n 10000 /path/sample.csv | csvsql -v -d ',' --db mysql://<user>:<passwd>@127.0.0.1:3306/test_schema --table my_test_table
`

The error message is copied below.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/vagrant/conda/envs/_test_csv/bin/csvsql", line 6, in <module>
    sys.exit(csvkit.utilities.csvsql.launch_new_instance())
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/csvkit/utilities/csvsql.py", line 188, in launch_new_instance
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/csvkit/cli.py", line 114, in run
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/csvkit/utilities/csvsql.py", line 94, in main
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/csvkit/utilities/csvsql.py", line 147, in _failsafe_main
TypeError: to_sql() got an unexpected keyword argument 'create_if_not_exists'

```
After some detective work, I was able to trace the issue to the version mismatch of one of its dependency package "agate-sql". In the "setup.py", it was declared like this:

```
install_requires = [
    ......
    'agate-sql>=0.5.1',
    ......
]

```

When I checked "agate-sql" version 0.5.1 on the Github: https://github.com/wireservice/agate-sql/blob/0.5.1/agatesql/table.py, the "to_sql" method signature looks like this:

`def to_sql(self, connection_or_string, table_name, overwrite=False, create=True, insert=True, prefixes=[], db_schema=None, constraints=True):
`

Only after I switched to the master branch https://github.com/wireservice/agate-sql/blob/master/agatesql/table.py, I can find the missing keyword argument "create_if_not_exists":

`def to_sql(self, connection_or_string, table_name, overwrite=False, create=True, create_if_not_exists=False, insert=True, prefixes=[], db_schema=None, constraints=True):
`

Therefore, I think this is a bug caused by adding a feature in csvkit that is not released by its dependent package.

issue_title: Parsing unit tests should not depend on pyparsing implementation details
issue_body:
Currently all of the unit tests which verify how the command line is parsed are tightly coupled with the details of the underlying pyparsing implementation.

These unit tests should be refactored in a way that they relate to how cmd2 parses the command line, but are not directly dependent upon this parsing being done by pyparsing in particular.  

Once they are more generic, they could be used to help safely refactor cmd2 to use a different library for doing this parsing.

issue_title: How to write the data of a dataset object to a file with python
issue_body:
<!--
If your issue is a usage question, submit it here instead:
- The imbalanced learn gitter: https://gitter.im/scikit-learn-contrib/imbalanced-learn
-->

<!-- Instructions For Filing a Bug: https://github.com/scikit-learn-contrib/imbalanced-learn/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#filing-bugs -->

#### Description
<!-- Example: Joblib Error thrown when calling fit on LatentDirichletAllocation with evaluate_every > 0-->
I look for importing a dataset (mammography) from the available collection of imbalanced datasets presented in [http://contrib.scikit-learn.org/imbalanced-learn/stable/generated/imblearn.datasets.fetch_datasets.html#imblearn.datasets.fetch_datasets](url). Thus, I tried to write the content of this data set in testfile.txt with a return to the line after writing each sample (Noting that a sample is composed of 6 values related to 6 features). I used `data.tofile` with `\n` seperator but the result's format shows a new line after each value and not each sample.
I tried also to convert the bunch object (`fetch_datasets()['mammography']`) to a dataFrame but this also fails because such object has not the `feature_names` attribute

#### Steps/Code to Reproduce
```
from collections import Counter
from imblearn.datasets import fetch_datasets
mammo = fetch_datasets()['mammography']
mammo.data.tofile("testfile.txt","\n","%s")
```
#### Expected Results
Write the content of the dataset to a file so that each sample is written in a new line

#### Actual Results

issue_title: Implement zerotier REST API
Negative strides in Python buffer view?
issue_body:
I have a C++ image type that allows negative strides. Python numpy arrays do too, so I was hoping I'd be able to convert between the two without data copy. But pybind11's `buffer_info` struct defines the strides with a `std::vector<size_t>`. In the code, Python's stride values are simply cast to unsigned:

    strides[i] = (size_t) view->strides[i];

This is bound to be problematic!

From Python's user manual (https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/buffer.html): 
>> Stride values can be any integer. For regular arrays, strides are usually positive, but a consumer MUST be able to handle the case `strides[n] <= 0`

I didn't have time to comb through the pybind11 code, are there any checks for negative strides?

Would it be OK if I tried to patch pybind11 to use signed strides?

issue_title: Feature request for exception translators
issue_body:
Pybind11 nicely converts exceptions derived from `std::exception` into corresponding Python exceptions. However it does not currently seem to support custom exceptions. In Boost Python this functionality is provided by exception translators (http://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_61_0/libs/python/doc/html/reference/high_level_components/boost_python_exception_translato.html).
What are your thoughts about adding equivalent functionality to pybind11?

issue_title: combine domain.tld and wildcard *.domain.tld in one certificate
issue_body:
I ran into an issue while testing the new wildcard feature of the acme-v02 endpoint in manual mode.
I was trying to create a certificate including a domain `example.com` and a wildcard `*.example.com`
Now the certbot runs the auth hook for each entered canonical name and THEN runs the validation.
So it's creating the TXT-Record for `_acme-challenge.example.com` for the first domain and then overwrites the same record with the second one before validating the domains and then the validation fails for at least one CN.
Is it possible do make certbot run the validations seperately, like: run auth-hook, validate, cleanup, run auth-hook, validate, cleanup?
Creating to different certificates with one CN `example.com` and the other one with `*.example.com` runs without any problems, but i would like to have both in one certificate if that is even possible.

## My operating system is (include version):
Tested on Ubuntu 14.04 and Ubuntu 16.04

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
Tested with certbot-auto and the one from the APT packet manager

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
Command:
```
certbot-auto certonly \
    --manual \
    --preferred-challenge dns-01 \
    --manual-auth-hook /my/manual/auth-hook \
    --manual-cleanup-hook /my/manual/cleanup-hook \
    --server https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory' \
    -d domain.com \
    -d *.domain.com
```
## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
As described above, it ran all the auth-hooks at first, then all validations, then all cleanup-hooks.
I expected it to run the first auth-hook, then the first validation, then the first cleanup, and then continues with the next canonical name.

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available):
###### Logs are stored in `/var/log/letsencrypt` by default. Feel free to redact domains, e-mail and IP addresses as you see fit.

## Here is the relevant nginx server block or Apache virtualhost for the domain I am configuring:
I deploy my certs manually, so the nginx server blocks are not relevant for this issue.

issue_title: Cert Management Improvement Project (C-MIP)
issue_body:
Based on previous discussions (#3587, #2071, #338), we should have an improved overall strategy for managing certs. This looks something like:

Step 1. list-certs command
Step 2. Specify canonical `{archive, live}` directories in conf file
Step 3. --use-lineage-name <lineage-name>
Step 4. --set-lineage-name <lineage-name>
Step 5. --use-lineage-name <wrong name or domain names or whatever>, "did you mean" based on filenames and such if ambiguous

Discussion items:
A. What should we call the canonical selector that I have temporarily termed "lineage-name"?
B. Where does this canonical name live?
Options for this include:
- as a line item in the conf file, whose name does not matter
- as the filename of the conf file, and document this choice.

I am personally for the latter, based on @schoen's observation that the file system then ensures uniqueness.

cc: @pde @bmw @cowlicks @PaulSD @yaegashi

issue_title: Getting a different error everytime on trying to obtain certificate for a domain
Make parser.py: add_server_directives documentation consistent with functionality
issue_body:
Documentation and at least one calling function assume that add_server_directives performs: "Add or replace directives in the first server block with names."

Instead, it add or replaces directives in all server blocks with names

issue_title: Wrong version gets installed
issue_body: If you run pip install jupyterhub-systemdspawner you get version 0.9.10 installed (see: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/jupyterhub-systemdspawner/0.9.10) which contains a bug with the conversion to the correct percentage e.g. 4.0 and 4 get converted to 400.0% and 0.12 gets converted to 12.0% (see: https://github.com/jupyterhub/systemdspawner/pull/24/commits/e127095988264caaf46c3f44b78203e04cf80576). The issue is easily solved by changing '--property=CPUQuota={quota}%'.format(quota=self.cpu_limit * 100) to '--property=CPUQuota={quota}%'.format(quota=int(self.cpu_limit * 100)) in systemdspawner.py

issue_title: Rest API unhandled exceptions should crash the node
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

After a lot of debugging, it was made clear that `gevent` already bubbles up unhandled exceptions up to the parent greenlet, which is the expected behavior. Except when the greenlet is spawned by the `APIServer`, where flask handles all exceptions and return `500` errors, where we want the node to crash. We must make sure the node also exits if any unhandled exception happens in the greenlet which handles a request.
Related: #2123 
Requires: #2171

issue_title: Use Event-Filters in raiden
Implement a queue of pending transactions
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

A queue of pending IO operations is required in order to make every state snapshotable, currently there is such a queue for [unconfirmed messages](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/transfer/state.py#L88), but there are not queue for pending transactions. This queue is required in order to safely implement snapshots.

#### TODOs

- [ ] Add a queue for pending transactions
- [ ] Add state changes to confirm transactions, these state changes must be emitted after the polling confirmed it and it's side effect is to remove the corresponding transaction from the queue.

issue_title: Use all global runtime signatures.
issue_body: Right now we are separating file signatures to global (`~/.sos/.runtime`) and local (`.sos/.runtime`) runtime directories. This was designed for cases when we would like to pack projects and their runtime information. However, with the dedicated pack subcommand, sos would be able to get signatures anywhere so there is no need to keep local `.sos/.runtime`. It therefore makes sense to put all runtime information to the global runtime directory.

issue_title: A reset/rerun magic?
issue_body:
Not sure if others also have this problem but I have found myself frequently need to go back to the front of the notebook to rerun a few cells to initialize the analysis or reset some variables so that I can start a new piece of analysis from fresh. This happens after

1. incorrect statements that mess up these variables
2. need to reset and start a new analysis
3. need to make sure certain key variables are correct

I am therefore wondering if we should facilitate such actions by something like

1. Mark cells that should be re-evaluated during reset.
2. Introduce a `%reset` magic to execute cells that are marked.

For 1, we can use the existing tagging mechanism so users can manually add a tag (`init`?) to such cells.
For 2, we could do `%restart --reset`, or `%dict --reset` or a dedicated `%reset` magic, but the behavior needs to be well-defined because we could restart subkernels, restart sos, reset sos dict in addition to running marked cells.

issue_title: Cannot use a pulumi.Output<string> to construct a resource name
issue_body:
The "name" argument for our resources is typed as `string`.  But this means you can't use an Output<string> to help influence the name of the resource.

I've run into this a few places now.  One real example is that in aws.serverless.FunctionOptions, we have a list of policies to apply to the lambda.  This is typed as `ARN[]`:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-aws/blob/master/overlays/nodejs/serverless/function.ts#L58

Ideally, this would be typed as `pulumi.Input<ARN>[]` so when I create a policy explicitly I can pass the Output<ARN> as part of the policy I attach.  However, this falls apart here:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-aws/blob/master/overlays/nodejs/serverless/function.ts#L135-L142

Where we use the policy's ARN as part of the name of the resource.

You could imagine playing tricks today, for example, doing something along the lines of getting the underlying promise from the output and then waiting for it to resolve and handling the case where it is unknown "somehow".

But that's a lot to expect folks to do.  I think we need some more first-class way of doing this.

issue_title: How to improve the myfreecams plugin
issue_body:
### Checklist
- [ ] This is a bug report.
- [ ] This is a feature request.
- [x ] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [ ] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description
There are so-called "No Mobile Feed" models in the MFC that are exclusively tied to the rtmp protocol and currently almost all MFC Recoders avoid or record them. 
Of course, they can be recorded but not using standard rtmpdump.exe, but using rtmpsuck.exe and most other systems that do not use standard rtmpdump.exe.
I know for sure that this could be done using python-librtmp (0.3.0) https://github.com/chrippa/python-librtmp/tree/master/librtmp

This is an old example. For a script to work, it should have fresh data for a particular "No Mobile Feed" model:
`import sys, librtmp, requests
url='rtmp://video483.myfreecams.com/NxServer'
cod=(438688409,'vywvjnwtihktlhgymnsmaxhxecrnoykn',19149701)
pp='mfc_119149701'
# Create a connection,
conn=librtmp.RTMP(url,connect_data=cod,playpath=pp)
# Attempt to connect
conn.connect()
# Get a file-like object to access to the stream
stream=conn.create_stream()
# Grab first chunk
data=stream.read(1024)
print data`

I have discovered that so-called "No Mobile Feed" have camserv <840 so it can easily be used to redirect to right method for stream recording

Operating system and version: Windows 7
Streamlink and Python version: 0.5.0 / 2.7.13

issue_title: AttributeError: 'Ticket' object has no attribute 'customer_id'
issue_body:
In our django-helpdesk app, we are allowing non-staff users the ability to update their tickets. However, when attempting to view the ticket (as a non-staff user), the following error is thrown:

`AttributeError: 'Ticket' object has no attribute 'customer_id'`

Looking at the "_is_my_ticket" function in staff.py, if a user is not superuser or staff, then a check is made to verify the user id matches the ticket customer_id. However, the Ticket model does not define "customer_id" as one of its attributes.

Reference: https://github.com/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk/blob/master/helpdesk/views/staff.py#L92

To get around this, the check has been changed to compare user.email to ticket.submitter_email

issue_title: Name conflict with Open Babel's Pybel
issue_body:
Hi there, I'm a developer of Open Babel and the author of its Python interface, also called Pybel. This has been around for a while - e.g. a paper back in 2008, "Pybel: a Python wrapper for the OpenBabel cheminformatics toolkit" (https://doi.org/10.1186/1752-153X-2-5).

We're starting to get support requests regarding confusion over "pip install pybel". We have "pip install openbabel" instead, which installs a pybel.py and openbabel.py. You can see where the confusion arises.

We can do a certain amount at our end to avoid the technical problem of a pybel.py versus a pybel directory, but we would appreciate if you would consider renaming your project to make it easier for users to find the software they are looking for and to avoid confusion.

issue_title: stream.aclose() not working properly?
issue_body:
When I modify the tutorial echo-client so that the sender closes the stream after one iteration, I expected the connection to close and the receiver to stop:
```python
# echo-client.py

import sys
import trio

# arbitrary, but:
# - must be in between 1024 and 65535
# - can't be in use by some other program on your computer
# - must match what we set in our echo server
PORT = 12345
# How much memory to spend (at most) on each call to recv. Pretty arbitrary,
# but shouldn't be too big or too small.
BUFSIZE = 16384

async def sender(client_stream):
    print("sender: started!")
    while True:
        data = b"async can sometimes be confusing, but I believe in you!"
        print("sender: sending {!r}".format(data))
        await client_stream.send_all(data)
        await trio.sleep(1)
        break  # stop after one run
    await client_stream.aclose()  # Close stream

async def receiver(client_stream):
    print("receiver: started!")
    while True:
        data = await client_stream.receive_some(BUFSIZE)
        print("receiver: got data {!r}".format(data))
        if not data:
            print("receiver: connection closed")
            sys.exit()

async def parent():
    print("parent: connecting to 127.0.0.1:{}".format(PORT))
    client_stream = await trio.open_tcp_stream("127.0.0.1", PORT)
    async with client_stream:
        async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
            print("parent: spawning sender...")
            nursery.start_soon(sender, client_stream)

            print("parent: spawning receiver...")
            nursery.start_soon(receiver, client_stream)

trio.run(parent)
```
The server prints a *connection closed* but the client’s `receiver()` does not detect the stream being closed and does not finish.

I also found no other way to explicitly cancel the `receiver()` task when the `sender()` task is done.

issue_title: A quick review
issue_body:
## String vs module

The library is currently identified using a string:
```python
library = current_async_library()
if library == "trio": ...
```
What about using the module itself?
```python
import trio
[...]
library = current_async_library()
if library is trio: ...
```

## Support for older curio/trio releases

Current  or older curio/trio versions won't be detected. 

Maybe have a fallback to detect them (e.g using `trio.hazmat.current_task()`)?

If not, it'd be good to list the compatible versions of the different libraries in the documentation.

## Set context var after sniffing when possible

If the current loop implements PEP 567, the context var can be set after sniffing to avoid performing the detection several times in the same task. 

PEP 567 support might be complicated to detect though.

issue_title: Inconsistent licence files
issue_body:
Following https://github.com/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/268#issuecomment-318916583

In 
https://github.com/python-windrose/windrose/blob/master/setup.py
it's `License :: OSI Approved :: CEA CNRS Inria Logiciel Libre License, version 2.1 (CeCILL-2.1)'`

but in https://github.com/python-windrose/windrose/blob/master/LICENCE
and in https://github.com/python-windrose/windrose/blob/master/windrose/version.py#L5
it's CeCILL-B

In https://pypi.python.org/pypi?%3Aaction=list_classifiers
CeCILL-2.1 is the only CeCILL license (moreover it's OSI approved)
so if we change to use CeCILL-B everywhere in this project we need to know what should be written in `setup.py` (maybe only `CeCILL-B`)

@LionelR Should we update update `setup.py` or `LICENSE` (and `version.py`)?

I noticed that some other projects have same "problem".
https://www.google.fr/search?q=Cecill-b+python+setup.py
https://github.com/d9pouces/django-floor
https://github.com/akaszynski/PyACVD
...

CeCILL-2.1 is a GPL compatible french license
CeCILL-B License is a BSD compatible french license (GPL incompatible)

https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Licence_CeCILL

CeCILL-B imposes author(s) citation not CeCILL-2.1

issue_title: Retain resource definitions between restarts
issue_body:
I may be missing something but I've gone through the docs and the other issues on here and I can't seem to find anything relating to this (not even a 'no, you can't do that').

What I'm looking for in essence is a way of being able to have an instance of localstack running  in docker without then having to go and (re)define all the necessary resources every time it starts. This is firstly so I can set up an instance once and then not have to go through and do it every time I restart, but also so I have a manner of being able to share a portable instance of these definitions for local dev/ testing across the team I'm working with.

I suppose one way would be to have a way of creating/ configuring resources as environment variables so I could set up and make something portable using `docker-compose` or what have you.  That way only a docker-compose definition would be required. 

The other way I tried (and failed) was to spin up an instance in docker, add the resources via the AWS CLI and then save the docker container as an image. That way I can check the image into our docker repo and have it pulled down again via docker compose. Again it doesn't retain definitions between restarts though.

Any thoughts on how I can achieve this?

issue_title: Non-pipe Docker calls turn parameter lists into strings without escaping, allowing arguments to wreak havok
issue_body:
https://github.com/DataBiosphere/toil/blob/27ec2ff4d357fe80957c70d97c48c52214deea2f/src/toil/lib/docker.py#L327-L335

This code turns nicely separated lists of arguments like `['rm', '-f', 'Not Important.txt']` into single strings like `'rm -f Not Important.txt'`, which can then be mis-parsed as something like `['rm', '-f', 'Not', 'Important.txt']`, with potentially disastrous results.

We need to use `pipes.quote()` (or `shlex.quote()` for Python 3) to escape each argument separately if we're going to gom them together into a single string, as we do for the pipe case:

https://github.com/DataBiosphere/toil/blob/27ec2ff4d357fe80957c70d97c48c52214deea2f/src/toil/lib/docker.py#L320

I noticed this when I tried to pass a nice, quoted, human-readble plot title with spaces in it to an R script running via Docker, and got only the first word of the title in the resulting plot.

┆Issue is synchronized with this [JIRA Story](https://ucsc-cgl.atlassian.net/browse/TOIL-335)
┆Issue Number: TOIL-335

issue_title: ZODB backend fails to remove triples
issue_body:
jus...@onebrightspace.com, 2009-09-01T09:29:08.000Z 
What steps will reproduce the problem?
1. Use ZODB backend.
2. Add a number of triples.
3. call remove((None, None, None))

What is the expected output? What do you see instead?
Expected: All triples removed
Actual: Only a percentage of the triples are removed.

What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?
2.4.1

Please provide any additional information below.
The ZODB backend uses IOMemory.  The remove() method calls triples() to get
a list of triples to remove.  The triples method yields the results from a
{}.keys(); which is normally a list in Python, however on a BTree this
results in an iterable object.  Because the remove() method is then looping
around an iterable list and not a static list, the size of the list shrinks
as items are removed, resulting in missed triples and general odd behaviour.

Fix is to explicitly list() the tiples call in remove:

for triple, cg in list(self.triples(triple)):

This may be a rather large list, but the alternative is to list() the
keys() call in the triples method.  The triple method is likely to be used
a lot more than remove, so forcing it to list everything on the keys() call
will slow it down.
